coredata.dc:title: Combining carbon dots with WO3-x nanodots for utilizing the full spectrum of solar radiation in photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Liwen
Chang, Qing
Cai, Tingting
Li, Ning
Xue, Chaorui
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Artificial photocatalytic systems represent a promising and available technology to directly convert sunlight into usable energy resources. Unfortunately, the current photocatalysts often absorb an only small range of sunlight spectra, hence greatly limiting the utilization efficiency of solar energy. Herein, we firstly developed a hybrid system that combines carbon dots (CDs) and oxygen-deficient WO3-x nanodots through a mild solvothermal method. This hybrid system features the heterojunction structure with dimensions smaller than 15 nm and can effectively utilize the full spectrum of solar radiation in photocatalytic oxidative reactions. In hybrid system, the CDs can enhance visible light harvesting ability of WO3-x nanodots and deliver their photoinduced carriers to the intermediate energy bands induced by oxygen-deficient of WO3-x. After further excitation with low energy photons, then more chemically useful carriers can be generated to participate fully in catalytic activation and reaction processes, resulting in 3.2 times higher photocatalytic activities of CDs@WO3-x than WO3-x nanodots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar energy utilization
Photocatalysis
Oxygen deficient tungsten oxide
Carbon dots
Heterojunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The role of LSPR-induced hot carriers in catalysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi
Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province ( 201903D421082 ), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM image of WO3-x, the inset is HRTEM image of a nanoparticle; B) TEM image of CDs@WO3-x; C and D) HRTEM images of typical heterostructures of CDs@WO3-x; E-I) EDS mapping of CDs@WO3-x.
A) XRD patterns of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x; B and C) XPS W 4f spectra of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x; D) UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x as well as solar spectrum.

A and B) Absorption spectra of OPD oxidation catalyzed by WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x with irradiation of simulated sunlight; C) Fluorescence emission spectra of OPD solution before and after irradiation of 4 min in the presence of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x, the inset showing the corresponding photos of OPD solution under 365 nm lamp; D) The comparisons of transformed amount of OPD excited by visible light and NTR light under the same conditions; E) Effects of light intensity on the transformed amount of OPD using WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x as photocatalysts; F) Relationship of light wavelengths with the transformed amount of OPD in the presence of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x.

A-D) Michaelis-Menten plots with varying concentration of OPD for WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x excited visible light and 500 nm light; E and F) Transformation rates of TBBQ to TBHQ in the presence of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x excited visible light and 500 nm light.

A and B) Absorption spectra of NBT solution over WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x irradiating with visible light and 500 nm light for 15 min; C and D) PL spectra of TA solution over WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x irradiating with visible light and 500 nm light for 15 min; E and F) Trapping experiments of reactive species during the photocatalytic OPD of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x; G) Transient photocurrent responses of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x under visible light irradiation; H) EIS spectra of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x.
A) MS plots of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x; B) XPS valence spectrum of WO3-x; C) Schematic of the electronic band structures for CDs and WO3-x.
The preparation procedures of CDs@WO3-x and WO3-x.

head.ce:title: Combining carbon dots with WO3-x nanodots for utilizing the full spectrum of solar radiation in photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Artificial photocatalytic systems represent a promising and available technology to directly convert sunlight into usable energy resources. Unfortunately, the current photocatalysts often absorb an only small range of sunlight spectra, hence greatly limiting the utilization efficiency of solar energy. Herein, we firstly developed a hybrid system that combines carbon dots (CDs) and oxygen-deficient WO3-x nanodots through a mild solvothermal method. This hybrid system features the heterojunction structure with dimensions smaller than 15 nm and can effectively utilize the full spectrum of solar radiation in photocatalytic oxidative reactions. In hybrid system, the CDs can enhance visible light harvesting ability of WO3-x nanodots and deliver their photoinduced carriers to the intermediate energy bands induced by oxygen-deficient of WO3-x. After further excitation with low energy photons, then more chemically useful carriers can be generated to participate fully in catalytic activation and reaction processes, resulting in 3.2 times higher photocatalytic activities of CDs@WO3-x than WO3-x nanodots.

ce:section.ce:para: Interest in photocatalytic synthesis has been inspired in part by the realization that sunlight is a green and sustainable energy source [1]. Nevertheless, the inherent energy band configuration of a semiconductor limits its light absorption spectral range within a very short wavelength region of solar radiation [1–3], causing a big challenge to utilize the full spectrum of solar radiation in photocatalysis. Tailoring the vacancies in the lattices of traditional semiconductor photocatalysts such as TiO2, WO3, has great potential for creating sub-band gap states below the conduction band [4–6]. Thus the electrons can be excited from the valence band (VB) and sub-band gap states to the conductor band (CB) or from the VB to sub-band gap states by lower energy photons, and so this leads to a considerably broadened absorption [5]. By introducing oxygen vacancies and further hydrogen treatment, for instance, the color of TiO2 can be tuned from white to black[7]. Although WO3 is also a nonstoichiometric oxide similar with TiO2, many oxygen-deficient tungsten suboxide (WO3-x) have stable crystal phase, not just surface reduced WO3 [8–10]. By localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects induced by oxygen vacancies, the WO3-x exhibits a strong near-infrared (NIR) absorption ability [8,11–15], but suffers from the deficiency of visible light capture.

Although the sub-band gap states can be generated by oxygen vacancies, let them become photocatalysis-active and they still have a favorable energetic location for the desired reaction and have sufficient lifetimes for excited carriers to migrate surface and participate in a photochemical reaction [4,7]. This implies that the increased light absorption cannot guarantee increased photoactivity. An essential prerequisite for achieving ideal photocatalytic performances requires highly efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced charges from sub-band gap states to avoid charge recombination in inside and surface [2,3]. To inhibit undesirable recombination of photoinduced charges, designing proper heterogeneous photocatalytic system has become an effective strategy towards the high charge-separation efficiency [2,3,16].

As a new class of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) with several nanometers possess broad visible light harvesting and multiphoton absorption abilities as well as excellent photoluminescence and other favorable properties, thereby promising diverse applications in photocatalysis, photovoltaic cells, energy storage, biomedicine, biosensor and others. [16–21]. The surface of CDs is always linked with numerous polar groups arising from the precursors and/or further functional treatment by heteroatom-contained substances such as NH3·H2O, (NH4)2HPO4 [22,23]. By the coordination reaction of surface groups with metal ions, the CDs could make for the nucleation reaction of metal-based compounds on their surface [16,18,24]. In particular, the CDs also have superior photoinduced electron transfer and electron reservoir properties [25,26], enabling them to efficiently separate and transfer charges upon light excitation through heterogeneous interfaces [16,23,24,27–30]. Accordingly, many hybrid photocatalytic systems have been constructed through the incorporation of CDs with various nanostructures or nanomaterials including of TiO2, WO3, and exhibited the improved photoactivities due to the roles of the CDs influencing the photoinduced charge behaviors of the systems [16–18,31–33]. But unfortunately few reported the coupling of CDs with WO3-x.

In this contribution, we firstly developed a facile and robust method towards the combination of CDs with WO3-x nanodots for a novel heterojunction-type structure with dimensions smaller than 15 nm (named as CDs@WO3-x). In this system, the CDs can enhance visible light harvesting ability of WO3-x nanodots and deliver their photoinduced carriers to the intermediate energy bands of WO3-x. Thereby, the full spectrum of solar radiation can be used to generate chemically useful carriers to participate fully in catalytic activation and oxidative reaction processes. The photocatalytic oxidative activities of WO3-x nanodots increase by 3.2 times after combination with CDs.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the original CDs were first prepared using selective oxidation of coal pitch with the mixed solution of H2O2 and HCOOH as we reported previously [34,35]. Subsequently, CDs were further modified with NH3·H2O following our already reported procedure [23]. Typically, 30 mg of CDs’ powders was dispersed into 40 mL of NH3·H2O solution (25 w%) by ultrasound treatment for 30 min, then transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor, which was sealed and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The final product was collected by reduced pressure distillation and dried in a vacuum at 60 ℃ to obtain the powders of amino-functionalized CDs.

For the preparation of CDs@WO3-x shown in Scheme 1, 17 mg of amino-functionalized CDs, 0.2 g of WCl6 and 2 g of oxalic acid were added into 40 mL of absolute ethanol. After vigorously stirring for about 30 min, 100 mg of NaBH4 were added in the resultant solution. After further stirring for 5 min, the final mixture was transferred into the same autoclave and maintained at 100 ℃ for 24 h and subsequently cooled to room temperature. The product was separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol three times. After drying in a vacuum at 60 ℃, the powder of CDs@WO3-x was achieved finally. For comparison, WO3-x nanodots were synthesized through the same recipe and procedure as those of CDs@WO3-x except for without adding CDs as shown in the bottom of Scheme 1.

Absorption spectra were performed on a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV3600) with an integrated sphere in the range of 250–2,500 nm wavelengths. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded on a Rigaku D/Max 2550 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of all samples were collected using a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source for identifying the composition and chemical bonding configurations of samples. The photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the samples were determined with a Bio-Logic (SP 120) electrochemical workstation using a standard three-electrode cell under visible light irradiation. Working electrodes were prepared by coating ethanol suspensions of the samples onto indium tin oxide (ITO) glass electrodes, which were then dried in a vacuum at around 80 °C for 2 h to evaporate the solvent. A platinum plate, Ag/AgCl reference electrode and 0.5 M of Na2SO4 aqueous solution were used as the counter electrode, reference electrode and electrolyte solution, respectively. The photocurrent responses of the samples under light-on and light-off conditions were measured at 0 V.

Light irradiation was provided using a solar light simulator (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT, China) with different filters. The light intensity can be adjusted through regulating operating currents and then confirmed by an optical power meter (CEL-NP2000-2, CEAULIGHT, China).

Photocatalysis of o-phenylenediamine (OPD): The characteristic absorption peak of the 2,3–diaminophenazine (DAP, oxide of OPD) is at 420 nm (ε = 16300 M−1 cm−1), so the changes in its quantity can be determined by the absorbance values. In the photocatalytic experiment, the photocatalyst was added into a 20 mL photoreactor containing 10 mL OPD solution of 5.0 mM. The resultant concentration of catalyst in photocatalytic reaction system is 0.2 g•L-1. The light intensity was set as 200 mW cm−2, and the absorption value of the liquid was measured every 5 min. The photocatalytic performance under different light sources and different wavelengths were operated by changing the filters, including AM1.5G, UVIRCUT420nm, CEL-QD500 nm, CEL-QD600 nm, CEL-QD700 nm, CUT700 nm, et al.

Photocatalysis of tert-butyl-1,4-benzoquinone (TBBQ): Similar with the above processes, the photocatalyst was put into a 20 mL photoreactor containing 10 mL TBBQ solution of 2.0 mM. The resultant concentration of catalyst in photocatalytic reaction system is 0.2 g•L-1. Then the absorption spectra of the liquid were measured after every 1 min reaction.

The kinetic parameters were calculated by Lineweaver Burk plots of Michaelis Menten equation:
V =
V
max
×
[ S ]
(
S
+
K m
)
where V is the initial velocty, V max is the maximal reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of substrate and K m is the Michaelis constant.

The turnover frequency (TOF) was calculated by the following equation:
T O F =
A o m o u t o f O P D
μ m o l
× c o n v e r s i o n
( % )
× s e l e c t i v i t y
( % )
M a s s o f p h o t o c a t a l y s t
g
× r e a c t i o n t i m e
( h )

In the first time, the photocatalyst was added into a 100 mL photoreactor containing 50 mL OPD solution of 5.0 mM. The resultant reaction solution with 0.2 g•L-1 catalyst was irradiated with an intensity of 200 mW•cm−2 for 10 min, and then its absorption peak value was simultaneously recorded using UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Hereafter, all of the reaction solution was put into centrifugal tube to centrifuge at 16,000 r min−1 for obtaining the solid. After drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C, the collected solid was then used in the second round.

The second cycle: The dried solid sample in the previous step was weighed. Correspondingly, a certain amount of 5.0 mM OPD solution was taken out. The used amount was calculated according to the proportion of the catalysts and solution in the first recycle. After irradiation for 10 min, the absorption value was then tested. Finally, the collection step was the same with the first time.
Similarly, the operation of the third and fourth cycles was as the same with the above mentioned.

Nitroblue tetrazorium (NBT) can be reduced by the ·O2 – radical to generate blue formazan and the characteristic absorption peak of NBT at around 260 nm can be detected with the UV–visible spectrophotometer. 1.0 mg of NBT was dissolved in 100 mL DI water with magnetic stirring for 30 min to dissolve completely. The NaOH solution with the concentration of 1.0 M was prepared, and 0.33 mL of the resultant solution was added into 10 mL NBT solution to be homogeneously dispersed with ultrasound. Then 1.0 mg of the catalyst was added into the prepared alkaline NBT solution. After 15 min irradiation under visible light, a certain amount of the solution was extracted to test the absorption spectra.

Since terephthalic acid (TA) can react with active hydroxyl group (·OH) and the production 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) possesses unique fluorescence at around 426 nm with excitation of 312 nm, we adopted this method to detect the presence of ·OH. The higher intensity of emission peak indicated that the more ·OH was produced. 0.02 g NaOH was dissolved in 50 mL DI water, and 0.0249 g TA was subsequently put into the solution to stir at 1000 r/min for about 2 h in the dark to ensure the completely dissolution. 1.0 mg of CDs@WO3-x or WO3-x was added into 10 mL of the prepared TA solution. A small amount of the solution was centrifuged after 15 min irradiation, and then supernatant fluid was taken to test the fluorescence.

To confirm light-to-heat generation by CDs@WO3-x and WO3-x, 10 mL aqueous suspensions with a sample concentration of 0.4 g•L-1 was taken into a 20 mL photoreactor and then irradiated with different wavelength lights under the given intensity (200 mW•cm−2). The increase of corresponding temperature in different time intervals were measured using a thermometer immersed in reaction solution. For comparison, only water (without any sample) was also irradiated with the same light source, maintaining all the aforementioned experimental conditions.

Based on a phenomenon that NaBH4 releases reductive hydrogen species gradually and slowly in ethanol solution [36,37], a one-pot reaction approach towards ultra-small size and water-soluble WO3−
x nanodots was successfully developed by virtue of the reducing capacity of hydrogen species [37]. This method is facile and robust as well as without needing H2 reduction treatment at high temperature comparing with other reported routes [8,12]. The combination of CDs with WO3-x nanodots is determined by the process of heterogeneous nucleation and growth kinetics [29,38], which is more complicated compared with single component nanoparticle formation. To obtain nanoscale heterostructures, it is effective to suppress early homogeneous nucleation by facilitating nucleation reaction on the surface of the other component nanoparticles [29,38]. Surface functional groups of CDs make them more readily accessible to transition tungsten ions through coordination adsorption [8], thus preferably inducing synthetic reaction on CDs under appropriate reaction conditions. Additionally, borohydride ions can react with CDs and transfer hydrogen species to them [39]. Therefore, the heterogeneous nucleation process may dominate in WO3-x synthetic reaction when CDs exist.

Fig. 1
A shows a representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of as-prepared WO3-x nanodots in the absence of CDs. It highlights a feature of ultra-small size with the diameter of merely several nanometers (size distribution shown in Figure S1A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset illustrates the interplanar spacings of 0.361 nm, corresponding to the (1 1 0) planes of tetragonal WO3-x. In contrast, the typical TEM image of CDs@WO3-x in Fig. 1 B reveals that it has larger size (size distribution shown in Figure S1B) than only WO3-x nanodots due to the growth of WO3-x on the surface of CDs. HRTEM images in Fig. 1 C and D illustrate that there are two kinds of structures in one nanoparticle. Therein, the part of WO3-x nanophase shows high contrast and clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.361 nm; the other part of CDs displays low contrast and imperfect lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) graphite plane. To ascertain the corresponding element compositions, we further collected energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping on CDs@WO3-x. Fig. 1 E − I show the uniform distribution of C, N, O, and W elements on the whole image. It is noted that the N element should originate from CDs since they have been modified by ammonium hydroxide.

To determine their crystalline structure, we performed X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x. From Fig. 2
A, their XRD peaks are well indexed to the nonstoichiometric tetragonal WO3-x (JCPDS No. 53–0434). As mentioned in TEM result analysis, there is no obvious XRD peaks assigning to CDs due to their poor crystallinity and ultrafine size (Figure S2), which is in accord with previous reports [40]. Not surprisingly, the results of infrared spectra were firmly supported the fact of the incorporation of CDs with WO3-x (Figure S3). To clarify the oxygen deficiency structures, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x were measured as well. The high-resolution XPS W 4f spectra for both samples in Fig. 2 B and C can be deconvoluted into six binding energy peaks. Thereinto, it can be found that both W5+ (binding energy peaks at 35.4 and 37.5 eV) and W4+ (binding energy peaks at 34.5 and 36.6 eV) species are presented in WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x, apart from W6+ species with binding energy at 35.9 and 38.1 eV for 4f5/2 and 4f7/2, respectively [13]. By direct observation of naked eyes, the resultant aqueous solution of WO3-x nanodots displays a blue color (Figure S4A), which is consistent with the WO3-x samples reported in literatures [8], because of the strong LSPR absorption ranged from 600 to 1100 nm as shown in Fig. 2 D. In contrast, as-prepared CDs@WO3-x presents a grey-green color owing to the distinction of its light absorption feature with WO3-x nanodots (Figure S4B). From Fig. 2 D, CDs@WO3-x shows the significantly enhanced absorption in the range of the whole visible light spectrum in contrast with WO3-x nanodots.

The catalytic oxidation of aromatic amines, e.g. OPD, has been widely used for a variety of applications including of detection of toxic metal ions, synthesis of conduction polymers, dyes and pesticides [30,41]. More importantly, the direct disposal of them into water will create serious environmental pollution. Taking OPD as a model molecule, here the photocatalytic activities of WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x were assessed under excitation with different wavelengths. OPD is a colorless compound and fairly stable without any photocatalysts [30]. Its main oxidation product is 2,3–diaminophenazine (DAP) that can exhibit orange–red color in aqueous solution with an orange‐yellow fluorescence [23,41]. The DAP concentration changes can be monitored using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy.

Fig. 3
A and B shows time evolvement of UV–Vis absorption spectra of OPD solution under simulated solar radiation (AM 1.5G) in the presence of WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x, respectively. Before irradiation, there was no any characteristic absorption peaks in UV–Vis spectra of OPD solution. After irradiation, the characteristic peak at 420 nm assigning to the formation of DAP was come out from UV–Vis spectra of OPD solution and increased with irradiation time as both WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x were present. Of particularly note is the absorbance that its increment resulted from CDs@WO3-x is markedly higher than that from WO3-x nanodots. Such results were also verified by the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 C, pure OPD solution can’t emit fluorescence, but its solution exhibited orange‐yellow fluorescence after irradiating for 4 min in the presence of the photocatalyst under simulated solar radiation. By contrast, the higher fluorescence intensity was generated using CDs@WO3-x as the photocatalyst. All of above results demonstrated that both WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x enabled OPD oxidation into DAP, but CDs@WO3-x exhibited higher activity to catalytic transformation of OPD under simulated sunlight. From Fig. 2 D, we deduced that CDs-improved absorbance in visible light range could endow CDs@WO3-x superior performance in photocatalysis. To confirm it, the catalytic activities of samples were also estimated in visible wavelengths (420–780 nm) and NIR light bands (>780 nm). Under the same irradiation time and light intensity, the characteristic absorbance at 420 nm resulted from WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x had a more striking difference under the irradiation of visible wavelengths than simulated solar radiation and NIR wavelengths (Fig. 3 D). What is particularly noteworthy is that the original CDs exhibit weak activities to catalytic oxidation of OPD under visible light irradiation as well (Figure S5A). By contrast, it can be found that the catalytic efficiency of the original CDs is much lower than that of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x (Figure S5B).

Furthermore, two control experiments were performed. Firstly, the different visible light intensities were employed to investigate the effect of photon number on the oxidation rate of OPD into DAP in the presence of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x. Fig. 3 E reveals that the absorbance of the produced DAP for both samples enhances significantly with irradiation intensity increase at the given irradiation time. Nevertheless, CDs@WO3-x brought larger increment in DAP yield than WO3-x nanodots under the same conditions. Secondly, we studied the influence of different wavelengths on the oxidation rate of OPD at the same irradiation intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 3 F, the transformation amount of OPD to DAP gradually increases with the visible wavelength increase for WO3-x sample, but this case is reversed for CDs@WO3-x. This behavior should be attributed to their visible absorption features shown in Fig. 2 D. More interestingly, the photocatalytic activity of CDs@WO3-x is also higher than that of WO3-x nanodots under laser irradiation with 808 nm or longer wavelengths (Figure S6). Accordingly, CDs should have another crucial role played in photocatalytic performance of CDs@WO3-x besides their contribution of light harvesting.

To gain further insights, the dependences of the oxidation rates on different concentrations of OPD were investigated on WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x excited using different wavelength ranges, revealing their enzyme-like behaviors based on their Michaelis − Menten kinetic curves [42,43]. From fitting plots, we further got the parameters of apparent Michaelis constant (K m) and maximal reaction velocity (V max) to describe the catalyst activity (Fig. 4 ). Under visible light irradiation (Fig. 4 A and B), K m is 1.57 and 1.16 mM while V max is 80.39 and 131.73 μM min−1 for WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x, respectively. In contrast, the V max of CDs@WO3-x is 1.63 times higher than that of WO3-x nanodots. To confirm it, we further calculated the turnover frequency (TOF) for the conversion of OPD under visible light irradiation. The TOF of CDs@WO3-x (1362 μmol /g·h) is 1.68 time higher than that of WO3-x nanodots (810 μmol /g·h), in accordance with the above result. Under monochromatic green light (500 ± 10 nm) irradiation (Fig. 4 C and D), the K m and V max of both samples reduce, but the V max of CDs@WO3-x is 3.2 times higher than that of WO3-x nanodots due to its stronger absorption at corresponding wavelength range. All above results indicate that CDs@WO3-x possesses higher affinity and higher activity for OPD in contrast with WO3-x nanodots. Moreover, both WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x did not show any decay in photocatalytic activities toward OPD after the fourth cycle of repetition test, which adequately testified their good photostability and durability (Figure S7).

The contribution of CDs on photocatalytic performance by means of visible light was further verified by another model catalytic reaction for transformation of TBBQ to tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) in the presence of WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x. From Figure S8, the typical peak for TBBQ reduces apparently in absorbance at 258 nm accompanying with a new peak increase in absorbance at 305 nm that can be assigned to the formation of TBHQ [44,45]. This reveals that the TBBQ molecules are gradually transformed into TBHQ with illumination time in the presence of WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x. Differently, CDs@WO3-x shows the higher conversion rate of TBBQ to TBHQ than WO3-x nanodots with both visible light and monochromatic green light excitation (Fig. 4 E and F). Nevertheless, the difference of both samples in conversion rate presents more apparent under monochromatic green light irradiation. This case is attributed to the enhanced ability to harvest green light due to the combination of CDs with WO3-x.

As previous reports [11–13,46,47], the reaction substrates may be preferentially adsorbed to the surface defect sites and surface functional groups on catalysts, in which are the preferably accumulated sites of photogenerated electrons or holes as well. Thus, the photoinduced carries can directly transfer from the photocatalyst to adsorbed substrates including of reaction molecules and molecular oxygen via these active sites [48]. The efficiency or quantity of delivering carriers to adsorbed substrates clearly decides the photocatalytic activation of reaction substrates [13,49]. To look into the traveling of photogenerated carriers, we firstly detected reactive oxygen species for WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x excited with visible light and monochromatic green light. Using nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) as a recognition agent [30], the yielded amount of superoxide ions (O2 •-) was estimated for WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x under the same irradiation time. Fig. 5
A and B depict that the characteristic absorption peak at 260 nm belonging to NBT declines for both samples under visible light and monochromatic green light irradiation. But it can be find that CDs@WO3-x is superior to WO3-x in the production of O2 •- species. The amount of •OH generated from WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x as the photocatalysts was probed using the reaction of terephthalic acid (TA) with •OH to generate 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH), which emits unique fluorescence at around 426 nm [50]. Fig. 5 C and D show photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TA solution in the presence of samples under visible light and monochromatic green light irradiation. It can be found that CDs@WO3-x also produces more amount of •OH than WO3-x at the same conditions. Fig. 5 E and F illustrate catalytic conversation efficiencies of OPD over both photocatalysts under visible light irradiation in presence of three kinds of trapping agents [30,50]. When p-benzoquinone (BQ, 1.85 mM) and Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 0.60 mM) were added into the photocatalytic system, the conversion rate of OPD decreased remarkably. However, the presence of isopropanol (IPA, 260 mM) had only a little influence on OPD oxidation. Hence, the h+ and O2 •- species should be the major reactive species in photocatalytic transformation of OPD for WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x.

To unveil the role of CDs in CDs@WO3-x, we firstly examined the influence of CDs on the electron transfer efficiency. As revealed in Fig. 5 G, the transient photocurrent responses of WO3-x nanodots and CDs@WO3-x in the same conditions are different. The photocurrent intensity of CDs@WO3-x is two times higher than that of WO3-x nanodots, suggesting that more photoinduced electrons from CDs@WO3-x are transferred to electrode. Another factor to facilitate charge transfer should be ascribed to the unique heterojunction structure of CDs@WO3-x. By electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the typical Nyquist plots can be obtained and reveals a markedly reduced charge transfer resistance for CDs@WO3-x system compared to only WO3-x nanodots (Fig. 5 H). Hence, the formed heterojunction in CDs@WO3-x endows it with a superior ability to suppress the possible recombination of photoinduced carriers inside.

The flat band potentials of samples were further measured through the electrochemical method [11]. As shown in Fig. 6
A, the Mott–Schottky (MS) plots of both the samples exhibit a positive slope, belonging to a typical n-type semiconductor. CDs@WO3-x reveals much smaller slope of MS plots compared to the WO3-x nanodots, suggesting the enhanced carrier density after incorporation of CDs. The carrier density (N d) was calculated from the slopes in the MS plots based on the below equation[11,30],
N d
= 2
(
e 0
ε
ε 0
)
- 1
d (
C
- 2
) / d V
- 1
where e 0, ε and ε 0 represent the electron charge, the dielectric constant and the vacuum permittivity, respectively. The carrier density of CDs@WO3-x (240 × 1021 cm−3) is dramatically enhanced comparing with WO3-x (138 × 1021 cm−3). Since all of their free-carrier densities are over 1021 cm−3 [30], the collective oscillations of a large amount of surface free carriers can induce the typical LSPR effect. This should result from the introduction of oxygen vacancies in WO3. By XPS valence band spectra, we determined the positions of valence band (VB) edge of WO3-x. As depicted in Fig. 6 B, its VB position (E VB) was confirmed to be 2.79 eV vs. NHE. Thus, the band energy diagram of WO3-x can be obtained by means of its flat band potential (Figure S9). Thanks to the presence of defect band states in WO3-x, there could be three kinds of channels for sunlight energy transitions: 1) Electrons excited from VB maximum to conductor band (CB) minimum; 2) Electrons excited from VB maximum to the empty defect band states below the CB; 3) Electrons excited from the occupied defect band states to CB bottom and/or by LSPR.

According to band energy structure of CDs (Figure S10), the energy level arrangements as well as the charge separation and transfer in CDs@WO3-x were illustrated in Fig. 6 C. Like it, the photoexcited electron transfer from the CB of CDs to the defect band states of WO3-x can occur in the heterostructure of CDs@WO3-x, while the holes from the VB of WO3-x are transferred to the VB of CDs preferably due to the difference of their VB potentials. Owing to the CB minimum of CDs lying more positive than the potential of O2 •- formation, CDs are unable to trigger O2 •- generation (Figure S11). Unexpectedly, more O2 •- species were observed from CDs@WO3-x than only WO3-x under the same conditions (Fig. 5 A and B). Figure S12 shows anti-Stokes PL spectra of WO3-x nanodots, implying the presence of the intermediate band states in their electronic structures [13]. It could be verified that the electrons transferred from CDs to the defect band states of WO3-x are hopeful to be excited by further absorbed photons, elevating their potential levels. Hence, the energy level alignments mentioned above not only result in the more efficient charge separation and the enhanced transfer efficiency for CDs@WO3-x, but also boost the carrier transitions from the defect band states to CB bottom of WO3-x through further harvesting visible and NIR lights, allowing more chemically useful carriers to participate in catalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of LSPR-induced hot carriers in catalysis
ce:section.ce:para: Hot carriers can affect the energetics of adsorbed reactants and improve the net reaction rate [51–53]. Energetic electrons formed on WO3-x in response to LSPR may populate unoccupied adsorbate orbitals producing the transient negative ion species. These species relax by releasing electrons back to WO3-x and endowing the adsorbate with vibrational energy [54]. Such vibrational energy deposited in the reaction coordinate is helpful to reduce activation barrier of the chemical reaction [52,54]. What’s more, the vibrational energy can bring heat to the product desorption since it is commonly endothermic [55]. It is reasonable to find that the sample activities of catalytic transformation of OPD to DAP are enhanced with the increase of reaction temperature (Figure S13). The photothermal conversion properties of WO3-x and CDs@WO3-x were accessed using irradiation of 500 ± 10 nm and 800 ± 10 nm lights (200 mW cm−2). As shown in Figure S14, the temperature of the sample solution with a concentration of 400 mg L-1 can be rapidly raised in large contrast to that of pure water similarly treated. Therefore, LSPR-induced heat energy also facilitated the photocatalytic performance of samples.

We offered an ideal candidate for effective utilization of the entire solar spectrum in photocatalytic transformation of organic molecules by coupling CDs with oxygen-deficient WO3-x nanodots. Our investigations revealed that CDs can induce synergetic effects on visible light harvesting and the efficiency of delivering photoinduced carriers by the nanometer heterojunction formed with oxygen-deficient WO3-x. The oxygen vacancies in WO3-x nanocystals bring about new intermediate energy bands that could match with the electronic band levels of CDs to form heterojunction-type photocatalytic systems. Thus, highly effective charge separation and transfer to surface take place in the system of CDs@WO3-x and favor more chemically useful carriers to participate fully in catalytic activation and redox processes under solar irradiation. Meanwhile, sufficient oxygen vacancy of WO3-x and surface functional groups of CDs also contributes to the adsorption and activation of reaction substrates. All of above results finally account for the superior phtotocatalytic performance of CDs@WO3-x with solar irradiation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China.
